strInstructions: Cut chicken thighs into bite-size pieces and slice the onion. In a pan, simmer dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. Add the onion and cook until slightly softened, then add the chicken and simmer until cooked through. Lightly beat the eggs without fully mixing the whites and yolks. Pour most of the egg over the chicken and onion, then cook until softly set. Add the remaining egg for a glossy finish. Spoon everything over hot steamed rice and garnish with green onion or mitsuba.

Slice the onion and prepare thinly sliced beef. In a pan, combine dashi, sake, mirin, soy sauce, and sugar, then bring to a simmer. Add the onion and cook until tender. Add the beef and simmer gently, skimming foam if needed, until the beef is cooked and flavorful. Serve the beef and onions over steamed rice with some of the sauce. Garnish with pickled ginger and green onions if desired.

Slice pork and onion. Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and a little grated ginger for the sauce. Sear the pork in a hot pan until lightly browned, then add onion and cook until softened. Pour in the sauce and simmer until the pork is glossy and coated. Serve over hot steamed rice and garnish with green onion or shredded nori.
strIngredient1: Thinly Sliced Pork Belly or Pork Loin

strInstructions: Cook ground chicken with sake, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, stirring constantly to break the meat into fine crumbles. In another pan, cook beaten eggs with sugar and salt over low heat, stirring to make soft scrambled egg crumbles. Blanch green vegetables if using. Serve the chicken soboro, egg soboro, and greens over steamed rice in separate sections.
strMeasure5: 1 tbsp for chicken plus 1 tsp for eggs

strInstructions: Cook diced chicken and onion in butter or oil until the chicken is cooked. Add ketchup and Worcestershire sauce, then cook briefly to reduce the moisture. Add warm cooked rice and green peas, then stir-fry until evenly coated. Shape the rice on a plate. Beat eggs with salt, then cook in butter until softly set. Wrap or place the omelette over the rice and finish with ketchup or demi-glace sauce.

Slice beef, onion, and mushrooms. Sauté onion in butter until softened, then add beef and cook until browned. Add mushrooms and cook briefly. Stir in flour, then add red wine, beef stock, tomato paste, demi-glace sauce, ketchup, Worcestershire sauce, and seasonings. Simmer until thick and rich. Serve the beef sauce beside or over steamed rice.

Sauté onion until soft and cool it. Mix ground beef and pork with panko, milk, egg, cooked onion, salt, pepper, and nutmeg. Knead until sticky, then shape into oval patties and remove air pockets by tossing between your hands. Sear both sides in a pan, then steam-cook with a lid until done. Make a sauce with ketchup, Worcestershire sauce, red wine, and butter, then spoon over the patties.

Cut potatoes, carrots, onion, and shirataki noodles. Sauté thinly sliced beef with oil, then add the vegetables and stir briefly. Add dashi, sake, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Simmer with a drop lid until the potatoes are tender and the liquid has reduced. Add green beans near the end for color and serve warm.

Mix grated ginger, soy sauce, sake, mirin, and sugar to make the sauce. Lightly coat thin pork slices with flour if desired. Sear the pork in a hot pan until lightly browned, then pour in the ginger sauce. Simmer and toss until the pork is cooked through and glossy. Serve with shredded cabbage and steamed rice.

Cut pork belly into large cubes and brown them in a pan. Simmer the pork in water with ginger and green onion to remove excess fat. Drain, then simmer again with dashi or water, sake, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and ginger until very tender. Reduce the sauce until glossy. Serve with soft-boiled eggs, mustard, or blanched greens if desired.

Whisk eggs gently without creating foam, then mix with cooled dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and salt. Strain the egg mixture. Place chicken, shrimp, mushrooms, and kamaboko into small cups. Pour in the egg mixture, cover, and steam gently over low heat until just set and silky. Garnish with mitsuba before serving.

Heat dashi in a pot without boiling aggressively. Add wakame and tofu and warm through. Turn off the heat or lower it, then dissolve miso paste into the soup using a ladle or small strainer. Do not boil after adding miso. Garnish with green onions and serve immediately.

Cut pork belly, daikon, carrot, burdock root, taro, konnyaku, and green onion into bite-size pieces. Stir-fry the pork and vegetables briefly in sesame oil. Add dashi and simmer until the vegetables are tender. Dissolve miso paste into the soup at the end and avoid boiling. Garnish with green onion and shichimi togarashi if desired.

Cook soba noodles in boiling water according to the package directions. Drain and rinse under cold running water until the noodles are chilled and the surface starch is removed. Drain well and serve on a bamboo tray or plate with shredded nori. Serve with chilled mentsuyu dipping sauce, chopped green onion, wasabi, and grated daikon if desired.

Prepare the sweet fried tofu topping by simmering aburaage in dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar until flavorful. Prepare the udon soup broth with dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and salt. Cook udon noodles separately, then place them in bowls. Pour over the hot broth and top with the seasoned aburaage, green onions, and shichimi togarashi.
strMeasure3: 3 cups for broth plus more for tofu
strMeasure4: 2 tbsp for broth plus more for tofu
strMeasure5: 2 tbsp for broth plus more for tofu

strInstructions: Prepare soba noodles according to package directions. Make a hot soba broth with dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Prepare shrimp tempura by coating shrimp in a light batter made with egg, cold water, and flour, then deep-frying until crisp. Place soba noodles in bowls, pour over hot broth, and top with shrimp tempura, green onion, and optional shichimi.

Mix flour, dashi, eggs, and grated nagaimo if using to make the batter. Fold in shredded cabbage and green onions. Heat oil on a griddle or skillet and spread the batter into a thick pancake. Lay pork belly slices on top, then cook until the bottom is browned. Flip and cook the pork side until crisp and the inside is cooked. Brush with okonomiyaki sauce and top with mayonnaise, bonito flakes, aonori, and pickled ginger.

Make a thin batter with flour, eggs, dashi, soy sauce, and salt. Heat a takoyaki pan and oil each mold well. Pour in the batter, then add pieces of boiled octopus, green onion, tenkasu, and pickled ginger. Turn the balls gradually with skewers until round and golden. Serve with takoyaki sauce, Japanese mayonnaise, bonito flakes, and aonori.

Loosen the yakisoba noodles and prepare the vegetables. Stir-fry pork belly until browned, then add onion, carrot, cabbage, and bean sprouts. Add the noodles and toss until heated through. Season with yakisoba sauce and stir-fry until glossy. Serve topped with aonori and pickled red ginger.

Mix ground pork with finely chopped cabbage, garlic chives, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, sake, salt, and pepper. Place a small amount of filling in each gyoza wrapper and pleat to seal. Pan-fry the bottoms until golden, then add water and cover to steam. Remove the lid and let the bottoms crisp again. Serve with soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chili oil.

Boil potatoes until tender, then mash while hot. Sauté onion and ground beef until cooked, then season with salt, pepper, and a little soy sauce if desired. Mix the beef and onion into the mashed potatoes and shape into oval patties. Coat each patty in flour, beaten egg, and panko. Deep-fry until golden and crisp. Serve with tonkatsu sauce and shredded cabbage.

Cook Japanese short-grain rice and let it cool just enough to handle. Wet your hands with water and rub with salt. Place rice in your palm, add filling such as salted salmon, tuna mayo, or pickled plum, then shape into a triangle or ball. Wrap with nori just before eating to keep it crisp.

Beat eggs with dashi, sugar, soy sauce, and salt. Heat a tamagoyaki pan or small skillet and oil it lightly. Pour in a thin layer of egg and roll it when half set. Move the roll to one side, oil the pan again, and add another thin layer of egg. Lift the roll so the new egg flows underneath, then roll again. Repeat until all egg is used. Shape with a bamboo mat if desired and slice.

Drain tofu well and cut into blocks. Lightly coat each piece with potato starch. Deep-fry or shallow-fry until the outside is crisp. Prepare the broth by simmering dashi, soy sauce, and mirin. Place tofu in a bowl, pour over the hot broth, and garnish with grated daikon, green onion, bonito flakes if using, and grated ginger.

Cut eggplants in half and score the flesh. Brush with oil and cook cut-side down until tender and browned. Mix miso, mirin, sake, and sugar to make a sweet miso glaze. Spread the glaze over the eggplant and broil or bake until bubbling and caramelized. Garnish with sesame seeds and green onion.

Cut burdock root and carrot into thin matchsticks. Soak burdock briefly in water to reduce harshness, then drain. Stir-fry the vegetables in sesame oil until slightly tender. Add sake, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, then cook until the liquid reduces and coats the vegetables. Finish with sesame seeds and optional chili flakes.

Soak dried hijiki in water until rehydrated, then drain. Cut carrot and aburaage into thin strips. Stir-fry hijiki, carrot, and aburaage in oil briefly. Add dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, then simmer until most of the liquid is absorbed. Add edamame near the end for color and serve as a side dish.

Drain chilled silken tofu and place it on a small plate or bowl. Top with grated ginger, sliced green onion, bonito flakes if using, and other toppings such as shiso, sesame seeds, or grated daikon. Drizzle with soy sauce just before eating. Serve cold.

Whisk eggs, sugar, and honey until combined. Add flour and baking powder, then mix into a smooth batter and rest briefly. Cook small pancakes on a nonstick pan until bubbles form, then flip and cook the other side. Sandwich sweet red bean paste between two pancakes and press gently to seal.

Mix glutinous rice flour and joshinko with warm water until a smooth dough forms. Shape into small balls and boil until they float, then transfer to cold water. Thread the dumplings onto skewers and grill or pan-sear until lightly charred. Make the glaze by simmering soy sauce, sugar, water, mirin, and potato starch until thick and glossy. Brush or pour the sauce over the skewers.

Coat the mackerel fillets with sake, then pat them dry. Sprinkle both sides with kosher salt and let the fish rest at room temperature for 20 minutes to remove excess moisture and season the flesh. Pat dry again, then place the fillets skin-side down on a parchment-lined baking sheet. Bake in a preheated oven until the fish is cooked through and lightly browned. Serve with grated daikon, lemon, and a small drizzle of soy sauce.

Mix miso, mirin, sake, and sugar to make the marinade. Pat the black cod fillets dry, then coat them with the miso marinade. Cover and marinate in the refrigerator for 2 to 3 days for best flavor. Before cooking, gently wipe off excess marinade so it does not burn. Broil or bake the fish until the surface is caramelized and the inside is tender and flaky. Serve immediately with rice and vegetables.

Slice the onion, carrot, and bell pepper thinly. Mix dashi, rice vinegar, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and dried chili to make the nanban marinade. Cut salmon into pieces, season lightly, and coat with potato starch. Fry the salmon until crisp outside and cooked through. While still hot, place the salmon into the marinade with the vegetables. Let it cool and marinate so the fish absorbs the sweet, sour, and savory flavors. Serve chilled or at room temperature.

Prepare the ingredients by boiling eggs, cutting daikon, blanching konnyaku, and preparing fish cakes. Simmer daikon in water until partly tender. In a large pot, combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and salt to make the broth. Add daikon, eggs, konnyaku, and fish cakes, then simmer gently until the flavors absorb into the ingredients. Let the oden rest if possible, then reheat before serving with karashi mustard.

Soak dried shiitake mushrooms in water, then reserve the soaking liquid. Cut chicken thighs and vegetables into bite-size pieces. Blanch or prep taro, bamboo shoot, burdock root, lotus root, carrot, konnyaku, and snow peas as needed. Sauté the chicken and vegetables in oil, then add shiitake soaking liquid, dashi, sake, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Simmer with a drop lid until the vegetables are tender and the liquid is mostly absorbed. Garnish with snow peas.
strMeasure4: 1/2 tbsp for chicken plus more for simmering
strMeasure5: 1/2 tbsp for chicken plus more for simmering

strInstructions: Prepare the sukiyaki sauce by mixing soy sauce, sake, mirin, and sugar. Cut napa cabbage, long green onion, tofu, shiitake mushrooms, enoki mushrooms, and shirataki noodles. Sear a few slices of beef in a hot pan with fat, then add some sauce and vegetables. Simmer gently, adding more beef and vegetables as you eat. Dip the cooked beef and vegetables into beaten raw egg if desired, and serve with rice.

Prepare a pot of kombu dashi and bring it close to a simmer. Arrange thinly sliced beef, napa cabbage, long green onion, mushrooms, tofu, and other vegetables on a platter. Cook the vegetables first, then swish each slice of beef briefly in the hot broth until just cooked. Dip the cooked beef and vegetables into ponzu or sesame sauce. Finish the meal with udon or rice cooked in the remaining broth.

Prepare the broth with dashi, sake, mirin, soy sauce, and salt. Cut chicken, seafood, tofu, napa cabbage, mushrooms, carrot, and green onion into serving pieces. Bring the broth to a simmer in a donabe or large pot. Add the ingredients in stages, starting with those that take longer to cook. Simmer until everything is cooked through. Serve with ponzu or the broth itself, and finish with rice or noodles if desired.

Prepare chicken pieces, napa cabbage, mushrooms, tofu, green onion, and other vegetables. In a pot, simmer chicken in water or kombu dashi to create a light broth. Add the vegetables and tofu, cooking until tender. Serve the chicken and vegetables with ponzu sauce and condiments such as grated daikon, green onion, and shichimi. Finish with rice or noodles in the remaining broth.

Boil the eggs, then peel and separate the yolks and whites if desired. Mash the yolks with Japanese mayonnaise, salt, sugar, and milk until creamy. Chop the egg whites and fold them into the yolk mixture. Spread butter on soft Japanese milk bread, add the egg filling, and sandwich the slices together. Trim the crusts and cut into halves or thirds.

Boil potatoes until tender, then mash roughly while still warm. Season with rice vinegar, salt, and pepper. Add sliced cucumber, carrot, onion, corn, and ham if using. Mix with Japanese mayonnaise until creamy but still slightly chunky. Chill before serving as a side dish.

Blanch spinach briefly in boiling water, then transfer to cold water. Squeeze out excess liquid and cut into shorter lengths. Toast sesame seeds, then grind them and mix with soy sauce and sugar to make the sesame dressing. Toss the spinach with the dressing until evenly coated. Serve chilled or at room temperature.

Cut kabocha squash into chunks and remove seeds. Place the kabocha skin-side down in a pot. Add dashi, sugar, sake, mirin, and soy sauce. Simmer gently with a drop lid until the kabocha is tender and has absorbed the sweet-savory broth. Let it rest briefly before serving so the flavor settles.

Soak Japanese short-grain rice and sweet rice if using, then drain. Peel fresh chestnuts and soak them briefly to reduce bitterness. Add the rice, water, sake, salt, and kombu to a rice cooker or pot. Place the chestnuts on top and cook without stirring. When done, remove the kombu, gently fluff the rice, and serve with black sesame seeds if desired.

Rinse Japanese short-grain rice and drain well. Prepare chicken, carrot, shiitake mushrooms, burdock root, and aburaage by cutting them into small pieces. Add soy sauce, sake, mirin, and salt to the rice cooker with the rice, then add dashi to the proper water line. Place the ingredients on top of the rice without mixing. Cook, then gently fluff everything together before serving.

Place hot steamed rice in a bowl. Top with flaked salted salmon, nori, green onions, sesame seeds, and wasabi if desired. Pour hot green tea, dashi, or ochazuke broth over the rice just before serving. Eat immediately while warm and soupy.
strMeasure3: hot, enough to pour over rice

strInstructions: Place freshly cooked hot Japanese rice in a bowl. Make a small well in the center and crack in a very fresh egg. Add soy sauce and mix quickly until the egg coats the rice and becomes creamy from the heat. Top with furikake, green onion, nori, or sesame seeds if desired. Serve immediately.

Prepare kanten jelly by dissolving kanten powder in water, simmering it, and letting it set in a mold. Cut the jelly into cubes. Prepare or gather sweet red bean paste, mochi or shiratama dango, fruit, and kuromitsu syrup. Arrange the kanten cubes, fruit, red bean paste, and dango in a bowl. Drizzle with kuromitsu syrup just before serving.

Make the batter by combining flour, baking powder, baking soda, sugar, egg, milk, and water until smooth. Preheat and lightly oil a taiyaki pan. Pour batter into the fish-shaped mold, add sweet red bean paste in the center, then cover with more batter. Close the pan and cook both sides until golden brown and crisp outside. Serve warm.

Whisk matcha powder with a small amount of milk until smooth. Heat milk, heavy cream, and sugar until the sugar dissolves. Temper the egg yolks with the warm milk mixture, then cook gently until slightly thickened. Stir in the matcha mixture and strain. Chill completely, then churn in an ice cream maker until creamy. Freeze until firm before serving.
